Exploratory metabolomic/longitudinal analysis of white tea

Tea is an important subject for me because it was the model crop on which my PhD thesis was based. At Tufts, we harvested tea grown in different seasons and elevations to see how its environment changed its flavor and health properties, all driven by its metabolism. Like many plants, tea uses chemical signals to communicate with its environment, since it can’t get up and walk away like an animal or even a microbe could. Evolution has driven myriad metabolic pathways to allow plants to defend themselves. This makes them particularly complex and challenging subjects of research, slowing studies on how crop qualities will change under different conditions, and exacerbated with the increasing threat and severity of climate change.

In this study, I selected two metabolites that change strongly with storage time. They correspond to the differential features 607.2477_411 and 130.0507_69, named as m/z-RT pairs.

For this demo/tutorial, I looked for data collected from Camellia sinensis samples on MetaboLights and found one study in progress by Wei and Li. This study aimed to investigate the sensory and chemical metabolites, both endogenous to the tea and exogenous from microbial contributions, over long-term storage. We will focus on the metabolomics data herein.

IDE considerations

I use VSCode with R and arf as the R frontend. Many people use RStudio or Python with marimo or Jupyter notebooks but this is what works best for me. I also manage R/Python environments with conda. For more info, follow Renkun Ken’s guide on writing R in VScode.

You’ll also notice I don’t really stick to the R style guidelines. I like my = signs more than typing out <- everytime because I learned on Python. It has never not worked for me. Conversely, I also like naming variables with . rather than snake_case or camelCase. It looks better to my eyeballs.

The conda environment yml can be downloaded here.

I’m also including a couple of packages from github, namely my own LCMStools and Jan Stanstrup’s xcmsVis. These should be installed with pak inside the R environment.

Finally, there’s a good chance if you don’t know your environment very well, you’ll end up crashing your R session consistently. Anecdotally, I found reducing the number of cores used for parallel processing leads to less memory consumption. I’m on an Intel Core Ultra 5 245K with 14 cores, and 32 GB of DDR5 RAM. Setting the MulticoreParam to 4 cores worked well for me, but 6 cores was consistently too much for my memory. There’s probably some way to lazy-load MsExperiment data, but we’ll cover that another time.

If you have problems, feel free to reach out at morimoto@motoanalytical.com.

Libraries

library(Spectra)
library(MsBackendMetaboLights)
library(purrr)
library(stringr)
library(dplyr)
library(curl)
library(LCMStools)
library(MsExperiment)
library(BiocParallel)
library(xcms)
library(xcmsVis)
library(plotly)
library(htmlwidgets)
library(colorspace)
library(webchem)
library(SummarizedExperiment)
library(ropls)
library(broom)

if(.Platform$OS == 'Windows'){
  register(bpstart(SnowParam(4, progressbar=FALSE)))
} else if(.Platform$OS == 'unix'){
  register(bpstart(MulticoreParam(4, progressbar=FALSE)))
}

Github packages

library(devtools)
pak::pak('jmorim/LCMStools')

For httpgd with VSCode and arf

library(httpgd)
hgd()
hgd_browse()

Loading the study data

First we load the study information from the MetaboLights repository using the MTBLS ID. This is my first time personally using MetaboLights, so I’m getting a view on how the data is organized.

mtbls.repo = 'MTBLS13336'
all.files = mtbls_list_files(mtbls.repo)
all.files
## [1] "FILES"                                                                       
## [2] "HASHES"                                                                      
## [3] "METADATA_REVISIONS"                                                          
## [4] "MTBLS13336.announcement.json"                                                
## [5] "MTBLS13336.mhd.json"                                                         
## [6] "a_MTBLS13336_LC-MS_alternating_reverse-phase_metabolite_profiling.txt"       
## [7] "i_Investigation.txt"                                                         
## [8] "m_MTBLS13336_LC-MS_alternating_reverse-phase_metabolite_profiling_v2_maf.tsv"
## [9] "s_MTBLS13336.txt"

Raw data is stored under FILES and study metadata in various formats in the root directory.

Below is the assay information table, also accessible online at https://www.ebi.ac.uk/metabolights/editor/MTBLS13336/assays. This contains sample names, data file names, and some experimental parameters. If the data were converted, we would also see a different file under the Derived Spectral Data File column, but the authors kept them in Thermo’s proprietary .raw format. Otherwise, using the Spectra package and the MsBackendMetaboLights backend, we could have loaded the .mzML files directly for analysis.

study.info = read.table(paste0(mtbls_ftp_path(mtbls.repo), grep('^a_', all.files, value = TRUE)),
    sep = '\t', header = TRUE, check.names = FALSE)

# Can use this when MetaboLights is down if already downloaded, which happened in the middle of this
#study.info = read.table(dir('.', pattern='^a\\_'),
#    sep = '\t', header = TRUE, check.names = FALSE)

study.info |> head()
##      Sample Name Protocol REF Parameter Value[Post Extraction]
## 1   NEG_0_year_1   Extraction                MeOH/H2O(4:1,v:v)
## 2   NEG_0_year_2   Extraction                MeOH/H2O(4:1,v:v)
## 3   NEG_0_year_3   Extraction                MeOH/H2O(4:1,v:v)

## 5 NEG_11_years_2   Extraction                MeOH/H2O(4:1,v:v)

## 1 Mass spectrometry                    alternating
## 2 Mass spectrometry                    alternating
## 3 Mass spectrometry                    alternating
## 4 Mass spectrometry                    alternating
## 5 Mass spectrometry                    alternating

##   Parameter Value[Scan m/z range]     Parameter Value[Instrument]
## 1                         70-1050 Thermo Scientific Q Exactive HF
## 2                         70-1050 Thermo Scientific Q Exactive HF
## 3                         70-1050 Thermo Scientific Q Exactive HF
## 4                         70-1050 Thermo Scientific Q Exactive HF

## 2           MTBLS http://www.ebi.ac.uk/metabolights/ontology/MTBLS_000660
## 3           MTBLS http://www.ebi.ac.uk/metabolights/ontology/MTBLS_000660
## 4           MTBLS http://www.ebi.ac.uk/metabolights/ontology/MTBLS_000660
## 5           MTBLS http://www.ebi.ac.uk/metabolights/ontology/MTBLS_000660

## 3 http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FIX_0000862                       orbitrap
## 4 http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FIX_0000862                       orbitrap
## 5 http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FIX_0000862                       orbitrap
## 6 http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FIX_0000862                       orbitrap
##   Term Source REF                     Term Accession Number MS Assay Name
## 1              MS http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/MS_1000484  NEG_0_year_1
## 2              MS http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/MS_1000484  NEG_0_year_2
## 3              MS http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/MS_1000484  NEG_0_year_3
## 4              MS http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/MS_1000484 NEG_2_years_1
## 5              MS http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/MS_1000484 NEG_2_years_2
## 6              MS http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/MS_1000484 NEG_2_years_3
##    Raw Spectral Data File        Protocol REF Normalization Name
## 1  FILES/NEG_0_year_1.raw Data transformation       NEG_0_year_1
## 2  FILES/NEG_0_year_2.raw Data transformation       NEG_0_year_2
## 3  FILES/NEG_0_year_3.raw Data transformation       NEG_0_year_3
## 4 FILES/NEG_2_years_1.raw Data transformation      NEG_2_years_1
## 5 FILES/NEG_2_years_2.raw Data transformation      NEG_2_years_2
## 6 FILES/NEG_2_years_3.raw Data transformation      NEG_2_years_3
##   Derived Spectral Data File              Protocol REF Data Transformation Name
## 1     FILES/NEG_0_year_1.raw Metabolite identification         NEG_0_year_1.raw
## 2     FILES/NEG_0_year_2.raw Metabolite identification             NEG_0_year_2
## 3     FILES/NEG_0_year_3.raw Metabolite identification             NEG_0_year_3
## 4    FILES/NEG_2_years_1.raw Metabolite identification            NEG_2_years_1
## 5    FILES/NEG_2_years_2.raw Metabolite identification            NEG_2_years_2
## 6    FILES/NEG_2_years_3.raw Metabolite identification            NEG_2_years_3
##                                                     Metabolite Assignment File
## 1 m_MTBLS13336_LC-MS_alternating_reverse-phase_metabolite_profiling_v2_maf.tsv
## 2 m_MTBLS13336_LC-MS_alternating_reverse-phase_metabolite_profiling_v2_maf.tsv
## 3 m_MTBLS13336_LC-MS_alternating_reverse-phase_metabolite_profiling_v2_maf.tsv
## 4 m_MTBLS13336_LC-MS_alternating_reverse-phase_metabolite_profiling_v2_maf.tsv
## 5 m_MTBLS13336_LC-MS_alternating_reverse-phase_metabolite_profiling_v2_maf.tsv
## 6 m_MTBLS13336_LC-MS_alternating_reverse-phase_metabolite_profiling_v2_maf.tsv
study.info[, c('Raw Spectral Data File', 'Derived Spectral Data File')] |> head()
##    Raw Spectral Data File Derived Spectral Data File
## 1  FILES/NEG_0_year_1.raw     FILES/NEG_0_year_1.raw
## 2  FILES/NEG_0_year_2.raw     FILES/NEG_0_year_2.raw
## 3  FILES/NEG_0_year_3.raw     FILES/NEG_0_year_3.raw
## 4 FILES/NEG_2_years_1.raw    FILES/NEG_2_years_1.raw
## 5 FILES/NEG_2_years_2.raw    FILES/NEG_2_years_2.raw
## 6 FILES/NEG_2_years_3.raw    FILES/NEG_2_years_3.raw
#specs = Spectra::Spectra('MTBLS13336', filePattern = '.raw', source = MsBackendMetaboLights())

Instead, we’ll download the raw data and convert herein. This will take a while, as high resolution mass spec data can be several gigabytes per file, so if you’re playing along at home, you can execute this block and go get a coffee. In this case, they’re ~80 MB per file. I’ve never worked with Orbitrap data so that’s pretty neat compared to Q-TOF profile data.

Note that I had to replace the FILES/ prefix from the repo because I don’t need that in my local copy.

I thought parallelizing this with walk would make it go fast but it does not. Probably faster to download a zip and unzip it locally.

paste0(mtbls_ftp_path(mtbls.repo), mtbls.repo, '.zip')
dir.create('_raw', showWarnings=FALSE)
download.file(
  url = paste0(mtbls_ftp_path(mtbls.repo), mtbls.repo, '.zip'),
  destfile = paste0(mtbls.repo, '.zip')
)

That didn’t work. MetaboLights doesn’t zip raw data in this repo. The curl package has a function multi_download that might work faster.

dir.create('_raw', showWarnings=FALSE)

response.table = curl::multi_download(
  urls = paste0(
    mtbls_ftp_path(mtbls.repo), 
    study.info[, 'Raw Spectral Data File']), 
  destfiles = paste0(
    './_raw/',
    stringr::str_replace(study.info[, 'Raw Spectral Data File'], 'FILES/', '')),
  resume = TRUE
)

response.table[,'error'] |> head()
response.table$destfile |> head()
dir('_raw') |> head()

This didn’t work either due to timeouts with the ftp server. In the end, downloading the files sequentially with download_file was most reliable.

dir.create('_raw', showWarnings=FALSE)
if(length(study.info[, 'Raw Spectral Data File']) != length(dir('_raw'))){
  walk(study.info[, 'Raw Spectral Data File'], ~ download.file(
    url = paste0(mtbls_ftp_path(mtbls.repo), .x),
    destfile = paste0('_raw/', stringr::str_replace(.x, 'FILES/', ''))),
    method = 'libcurl'
  )
}

Data conversion

To read files with xcms, data need to be converted to .mzML format with MSConvert from the ProteoWizard package. Since I’m on Linux, I need a docker container to run this with a Windows image. On Windows, it can run natively. The code below detects the host environment and chooses the appropriate conversion command. The convertDtoMZML function from my LCMStools package was built for Agilent .d files, but it should work for .raw files too since the parameters aren’t too specific. Docker will prompt for a password for sudo access. By using the Docker image, you agree to certain vendor licenses for converting proprietary formats to .mzML.

raw.files = dir('_raw', pattern = '\\.raw$', full.names = TRUE)

# Check if length mzML files matches length raw files
if(length(raw.files) > length(dir('_mzML', pattern = '\\.mzML$'))){
  if(.Platform$OS == 'Windows'){
    raw.files |> walk(LCMStools::convertDtoMZML, path.out = '_mzML')
  } else if(.Platform$OS == 'unix'){
    walk(raw.files, ~ system(paste0(
      'sudo docker run -it --rm -v $PWD:/data proteowizard/pwiz-skyline-i-agree-to-the-vendor-licenses:latest wine msconvert /data/_raw/', basename(.), ' --filter "peakPicking true 1-" -o /data/_mzML --mzML'
      )
    ))
  }
}

mzml.files = dir('_mzML', pattern = '\\.mzML$', full.names = TRUE)

Initial data inspection

First we’ll plot the positive TIC to make sure there aren’t any misbehaving data files or sample runs. In this case, I’m only going to focus on the positive data for now and leave the negative data as an exercise for the reader.

mzml.files |> head()
## [1] "_mzML/NEG_0_year_1.mzML"   "_mzML/NEG_0_year_2.mzML"  
## [3] "_mzML/NEG_0_year_3.mzML"   "_mzML/NEG_11_years_1.mzML"
## [5] "_mzML/NEG_11_years_2.mzML" "_mzML/NEG_11_years_3.mzML"
pos.files = mzml.files[str_detect(mzml.files, 'POS')]
pos.data = readMsExperiment(pos.files)

plot(chromatogram(pos.data), main = 'Positive TIC')

It looks like nothing disastrous happened in the data collection. From the TICs, it’s hard to tell right away if there are any differences since the noise buries most of the valuable data. Sometimes you can get away with focusing on a certain region that’s obviously different.

Looking closer, the polar region before 100 seconds in retention changes with intensity. I can infer that these are polar compounds since the column used was a Waters Acquity bridged-ethylene hybrid (BEH) C18 column, which is reversed phase. Polar compounds elute first, and nonpolar compounds elute later.

Supplying readMsExperiment with metadata

Now that we’ve taken a first look at the data, I’m going to go back and supply the readMsExperiment function with the relevant sampleData information so we can use it downstream for peak grouping and statistical analyses. The original MsExperiment objects will be replaced so they don’t take up more memory.

Conveniently, we can build out sampleData from the assay data from MetaboLights. dplyr can filter the study.info table to split it into the positive and negative ion mode information, and stringr can extract the year value as a separate longitudinal variable. Sometimes, another package will override the filter function definition from dplyr, so it’s good to scope it to the right one. The study.info table also has columns with duplicate names that need to be renamed or removed to work with dplyr.

study.info |> head()
##      Sample Name Protocol REF Parameter Value[Post Extraction]
## 1   NEG_0_year_1   Extraction                MeOH/H2O(4:1,v:v)
## 2   NEG_0_year_2   Extraction                MeOH/H2O(4:1,v:v)
## 3   NEG_0_year_3   Extraction                MeOH/H2O(4:1,v:v)
## 4 NEG_11_years_1   Extraction                MeOH/H2O(4:1,v:v)
## 5 NEG_11_years_2   Extraction                MeOH/H2O(4:1,v:v)
## 6 NEG_11_years_3   Extraction                MeOH/H2O(4:1,v:v)
##   Parameter Value[Derivatization]  Extract Name   Protocol REF
## 1                            none  NEG_0_year_1 Chromatography
## 2                            none  NEG_0_year_2 Chromatography
## 3                            none  NEG_0_year_3 Chromatography
## 4                            none NEG_2_years_1 Chromatography
## 5                            none NEG_2_years_2 Chromatography
## 6                            none NEG_2_years_3 Chromatography
##   Parameter Value[Chromatography Instrument] Term Source REF
## 1     Thermo Scientific LTQ Orbitrap Classic           MTBLS
## 2     Thermo Scientific LTQ Orbitrap Classic           MTBLS
## 3     Thermo Scientific LTQ Orbitrap Classic           MTBLS
## 4     Thermo Scientific LTQ Orbitrap Classic           MTBLS
## 5     Thermo Scientific LTQ Orbitrap Classic           MTBLS

## 1 http://www.ebi.ac.uk/metabolights/ontology/MTBLS_000645
## 2 http://www.ebi.ac.uk/metabolights/ontology/MTBLS_000645

## 5 http://www.ebi.ac.uk/metabolights/ontology/MTBLS_000645
## 6 http://www.ebi.ac.uk/metabolights/ontology/MTBLS_000645
##   Parameter Value[Autosampler model]
## 1                                 NA
## 2                                 NA
## 3                                 NA
## 4                                 NA
## 5                                 NA
## 6                                 NA
##                       Parameter Value[Column model]
## 1 ACQUITY UPLC BEH C18(1.7 um,2.1 mm x150mm;Waters)

## 1                reverse phase

## 1 ACQUITY UPLC BEH C18 VanGuard Pre-column(1.7 um, 2.1 mmx5mm;Waters)

##   Parameter Value[Scan m/z range]     Parameter Value[Instrument]
## 1                         70-1050 Thermo Scientific Q Exactive HF

## 3                         70-1050 Thermo Scientific Q Exactive HF

##   Term Source REF                                   Term Accession Number
## 1           MTBLS http://www.ebi.ac.uk/metabolights/ontology/MTBLS_000660

## 5 ion spray ionization mass spectrometry             FIX
## 6 ion spray ionization mass spectrometry             FIX
##                        Term Accession Number Parameter Value[Mass analyzer]
## 1 http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FIX_0000862                       orbitrap
## 2 http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FIX_0000862                       orbitrap
## 3 http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FIX_0000862                       orbitrap
## 4 http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FIX_0000862                       orbitrap
## 5 http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FIX_0000862                       orbitrap
## 6 http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FIX_0000862                       orbitrap
##   Term Source REF                     Term Accession Number MS Assay Name
## 1              MS http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/MS_1000484  NEG_0_year_1
## 2              MS http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/MS_1000484  NEG_0_year_2
## 3              MS http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/MS_1000484  NEG_0_year_3
## 4              MS http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/MS_1000484 NEG_2_years_1
## 5              MS http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/MS_1000484 NEG_2_years_2
## 6              MS http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/MS_1000484 NEG_2_years_3
##    Raw Spectral Data File        Protocol REF Normalization Name
## 1  FILES/NEG_0_year_1.raw Data transformation       NEG_0_year_1
## 2  FILES/NEG_0_year_2.raw Data transformation       NEG_0_year_2
## 3  FILES/NEG_0_year_3.raw Data transformation       NEG_0_year_3
## 4 FILES/NEG_2_years_1.raw Data transformation      NEG_2_years_1
## 5 FILES/NEG_2_years_2.raw Data transformation      NEG_2_years_2
## 6 FILES/NEG_2_years_3.raw Data transformation      NEG_2_years_3
##   Derived Spectral Data File              Protocol REF Data Transformation Name
## 1     FILES/NEG_0_year_1.raw Metabolite identification         NEG_0_year_1.raw
## 2     FILES/NEG_0_year_2.raw Metabolite identification             NEG_0_year_2
## 3     FILES/NEG_0_year_3.raw Metabolite identification             NEG_0_year_3
## 4    FILES/NEG_2_years_1.raw Metabolite identification            NEG_2_years_1
## 5    FILES/NEG_2_years_2.raw Metabolite identification            NEG_2_years_2
## 6    FILES/NEG_2_years_3.raw Metabolite identification            NEG_2_years_3
##                                                     Metabolite Assignment File
## 1 m_MTBLS13336_LC-MS_alternating_reverse-phase_metabolite_profiling_v2_maf.tsv
## 2 m_MTBLS13336_LC-MS_alternating_reverse-phase_metabolite_profiling_v2_maf.tsv
## 3 m_MTBLS13336_LC-MS_alternating_reverse-phase_metabolite_profiling_v2_maf.tsv
## 4 m_MTBLS13336_LC-MS_alternating_reverse-phase_metabolite_profiling_v2_maf.tsv
## 5 m_MTBLS13336_LC-MS_alternating_reverse-phase_metabolite_profiling_v2_maf.tsv
## 6 m_MTBLS13336_LC-MS_alternating_reverse-phase_metabolite_profiling_v2_maf.tsv
study.info.m = study.info
colnames(study.info.m) = make.unique(colnames(study.info.m))
study.info.m = study.info.m |>
  mutate(
    polarity = str_extract(`Sample Name`, 'POS|NEG'),
    year = str_extract(`Sample Name`, '\\_\\d+\\_') |> str_replace_all('\\_', '') |> as.numeric()
  )
pos.data = readMsExperiment(pos.files, 
  sampleData = study.info.m |> filter(polarity == 'POS'))

Peak picking

The next step is to get some starting parameters for peak picking. First, we’ll zoom in on the early and late parts to refine the retention time window on which peak picking will operate. xcmsVis provides ggplot version of xcms plots, and plotly enables interactions, although it’s slower than generating static plots, so I’m not going to incorporate it into my knitr output.

gplot(chromatogram(pos.data)) |> ggplotly()

Zooming in, chromatographic peaks start eluting at 25 s, which is the dead time. This is the time it takes for unretained sample to pass through the column and reach the detector. It can be determined based on the column geometry and flow rate. For example, in this study, the authors used a 2.1 \(\times\) 100 mm column with a 5 mm guard column (notice the discrepancy in the study.info and the Chromatography section of the protocol info), but didn’t specify the flow rate. These columns are usually run at 0.1 – 0.2 mL/min. Let’s check using the observed dead time. Column volume can be estimated, depending on the porosity, as \(0.7 \times \pi r^2 L\), where \(r\) is the column radius (half the column diameter noted above) and \(L\) is the column length. Note this ignores any dead volume contributions from extra-column tubing.

dead.time = 25
col.vol = 0.7 * pi * (2.1/2)^2 * 105
est.flow.rate = 60 * col.vol / dead.time
print(est.flow.rate)
## [1] 610.9801

I was a bit off. It looks like they used a flow rate of 0.500 – 0.600 mL/min, but that’s not unheard of in ultra-high performance liquid chromatography (UHPLC).

At the other end of the chromatograms, it looks like a diverter valve was used to redirect the stream when the gradient was done, and I don’t see any artifacts, so the retention window for peak picking will be c(25, 480). Since the same chromatographic method was used, this window should work for the negative mode data too.

Before delving into peak picking, we need to set up colors for plotting. I use colorspace to generate my palettes. For longitudinal studies, a sequential palette is appropriate. Simple A versus B studies can use diverging palettes.

year.levels = sort(unique(study.info.m$year))
year.colors = sequential_hcl(length(year.levels), 'Viridis')
names(year.colors) = sprintf('%02d', year.levels)

# QC (NA year) gets its own neutral color appended separately, rather than
# occupying a slot in the year gradient. 'NA' starts with a letter, so it
# always alphabetically sorts after any 2-digit zero-padded number.
year.colors['NA'] = '#7f7f7f'

Now to peak picking.

Manually looking at the plotly TIC, the biggest peak (between 207 and 223 s, probably caffeine) had a peakwidth of about 16 seconds, so I’ll round up to 20 for the max peakwidth. A smaller peak had a peakwidth of around 8 seconds, so I’ll round down to 5 for the min peakwidth. The ppm parameter can usually be estimated by the instrument type. For TOFs and Q-TOFs, I’ve had to set this between 20 – 80 ppm for good peak detection. Orbitraps are much more accurate on the m/z domain, so 10 ppm should be sufficient.

The snr parameter won’t be touched because it’s shown to not be particularly useful here: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12859-023-05533-4.

cwp = CentWaveParam(
  ppm = 10,
  peakwidth = c(5, 20)
)

peaks.pos = findChromPeaks(pos.data |> filterRt(c(25, 480)), param = cwp)

dir.create('_rds', showWarnings = FALSE)
saveRDS(peaks.pos, '_rds/peaks_pos.rds')

Since the biggest peak in tea is probably caffeine in positive mode, we’ll use that to test how peak picking went. We can use webchem to look up the monoisotopic mass on PubChem, then calculate the theoretical m/z for the [M+H]+ ion.

caffeine.cid = webchem::get_cid('caffeine')
caffeine.mass = webchem::pc_prop(caffeine.cid |> pull(cid), 'MonoisotopicMass')
caffeine.mz = as.numeric(caffeine.mass$MonoisotopicMass) + 1.00728
caffeine.mzr = LCMStools::calcPPM(caffeine.mz, 10)

Peak coloring can get tricky because the number of peaks often is greater than the number of chromatograms to draw, so there’s some complicated subsetting required. I’ll break it down as best as I can below.

# We want to color the samples by how many years they were stored.
sampleData(peaks.pos)$year
##  [1]  0  0  0 11 11 11  2  2  2  5  5  5  7  7  7  9  9  9 NA NA NA NA
# There are 22 samples in this set, with 7 different "year" conditions,
# including NA for the QC's
year.colors
##        00        02        05        07        09        11        NA 
## "#4B0055" "#274983" "#008298" "#00B28A" "#7ED357" "#FDE333" "#7f7f7f"
# This will build out a list of what color each sample will be based on year
caffeine.colors =
  year.colors[str_replace_na(sprintf('%02d',sampleData(peaks.pos)$year))]
caffeine.colors
##        00        00        00        11        11        11        02        02 
## "#4B0055" "#4B0055" "#4B0055" "#FDE333" "#FDE333" "#FDE333" "#274983" "#274983" 
##        02        05        05        05        07        07        07        09 
## "#274983" "#008298" "#008298" "#008298" "#00B28A" "#00B28A" "#00B28A" "#7ED357" 
##        09        09        NA        NA        NA        NA 
## "#7ED357" "#7ED357" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f"
# The peaks need to be filtered by the caffeine _m/z_, otherwise we'll be
# getting back many more peaks from different ions
chromPeaks(peaks.pos |>
  filterMz(c(caffeine.mzr[1], caffeine.mzr[2])))[,'sample']
## CP010851 CP010852 CP021684 CP021685 CP032582 CP032583 CP043627 CP054605 
##        1        1        2        2        3        3        4        5 
## CP065557 CP065558 CP076478 CP076479 CP087256 CP087257 CP098054 CP098055 
##        6        6        7        7        8        8        9        9 
## CP109249 CP120395 CP131752 CP142775 CP142776 CP153870 CP164662 CP175595 
##       10       11       12       13       13       14       15       16 
## CP175596 CP186560 CP186561 CP197535 CP197536 CP208488 CP208489 CP219462 
##       16       17       17       18       18       19       19       20 
## CP230272 CP230273 CP241261 CP241262 
##       21       21       22       22
# Those peaks need to be mapped by which sample they are from to the
# list of caffeine colors
caffeine.colors[chromPeaks(peaks.pos |>
  filterMz(c(caffeine.mzr[1], caffeine.mzr[2])))[,'sample']]
##        00        00        00        00        00        00        11        11 
## "#4B0055" "#4B0055" "#4B0055" "#4B0055" "#4B0055" "#4B0055" "#FDE333" "#FDE333" 
##        11        11        02        02        02        02        02        02 
## "#FDE333" "#FDE333" "#274983" "#274983" "#274983" "#274983" "#274983" "#274983" 
##        05        05        05        07        07        07        07        09 
## "#008298" "#008298" "#008298" "#00B28A" "#00B28A" "#00B28A" "#00B28A" "#7ED357" 
##        09        09        09        09        09        NA        NA        NA 
## "#7ED357" "#7ED357" "#7ED357" "#7ED357" "#7ED357" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f" 
##        NA        NA        NA        NA 
## "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f"
# For peak backgrounds, the hex color needs an alpha value appended to it
# for transparency so it's easier to see overlaid peaks
paste0(caffeine.colors[chromPeaks(peaks.pos |>
  filterMz(c(caffeine.mzr[1], caffeine.mzr[2])))[,'sample']], '10')
##  [1] "#4B005510" "#4B005510" "#4B005510" "#4B005510" "#4B005510" "#4B005510"
##  [7] "#FDE33310" "#FDE33310" "#FDE33310" "#FDE33310" "#27498310" "#27498310"
## [13] "#27498310" "#27498310" "#27498310" "#27498310" "#00829810" "#00829810"
## [19] "#00829810" "#00B28A10" "#00B28A10" "#00B28A10" "#00B28A10" "#7ED35710"
## [25] "#7ED35710" "#7ED35710" "#7ED35710" "#7ED35710" "#7ED35710" "#7f7f7f10"
## [31] "#7f7f7f10" "#7f7f7f10" "#7f7f7f10" "#7f7f7f10" "#7f7f7f10" "#7f7f7f10"
# Sometimes I like to make this alpha value dynamic based on the number of
# samples. Since there are 22, dividing by a total opacity (if everything's overlaid)
# by number of samples, rounded down, yields 4% opacity. Since this is only 1
# digit, it needs to be padded for the hex color string.
caffeine.peakBg = paste0(
  caffeine.colors[chromPeaks(peaks.pos |>
  filterMz(c(caffeine.mzr[1], caffeine.mzr[2])))[,'sample']], 
  sprintf('%02d', floor(90 / length(caffeine.colors))))
caffeine.peakBg
##  [1] "#4B005504" "#4B005504" "#4B005504" "#4B005504" "#4B005504" "#4B005504"
##  [7] "#FDE33304" "#FDE33304" "#FDE33304" "#FDE33304" "#27498304" "#27498304"
## [13] "#27498304" "#27498304" "#27498304" "#27498304" "#00829804" "#00829804"
## [19] "#00829804" "#00B28A04" "#00B28A04" "#00B28A04" "#00B28A04" "#7ED35704"
## [25] "#7ED35704" "#7ED35704" "#7ED35704" "#7ED35704" "#7ED35704" "#7f7f7f04"
## [31] "#7f7f7f04" "#7f7f7f04" "#7f7f7f04" "#7f7f7f04" "#7f7f7f04" "#7f7f7f04"
# Ultimately, for coloring chromatograms and peaks, we end up with two lists:
# one for the ion current line (caffeine.colors), and one for the integrated
# peak background (caffeine.peakBg)

peaks.pos |> 
  filterMz(c(caffeine.mzr[1], caffeine.mzr[2])) |>
  chromatogram() |> 
  plot(col = caffeine.colors, 
    peakBg = caffeine.peakBg,
    main = 'Caffeine peak results with initial cwp parameters')

The big split in the peak down the middle shows we need to refine these parameters more. The peakwidth minimum and maximum should be increased so there’s no peak splitting. We can also check the mass accuracy drift from the theoretical caffeine m/z.

chromPeaks(peaks.pos |> filterMz(c(caffeine.mzr[1], caffeine.mzr[2]))) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  mutate(
    peakwidth = rtmax - rtmin,
    mz.error = abs(mz - caffeine.mz),
    ppm.error = 10^6 * (mz.error / caffeine.mz)
  )
##                mz    mzmin    mzmax       rt    rtmin    rtmax         into
## CP010851 195.0885 195.0885 195.0886 210.8320 206.5829 213.7420 104396560794
## CP010852 195.0885 195.0885 195.0886 215.1727 213.7420 220.3318  67637979037
## CP021684 195.0885 195.0885 195.0886 211.2274 206.3626 213.7749 104069121504
## CP021685 195.0886 195.0885 195.0886 215.5178 213.7749 220.3354  75693973931
## CP032582 195.0886 195.0886 195.0887 211.0636 204.9588 221.8932 201710102115
## CP032583 195.0886 195.0885 195.0886 215.2933 213.4622 220.4746  82847652894
## CP043627 195.0887 195.0886 195.0887 211.1244 206.4141 220.2145 162021492620
## CP054605 195.0887 195.0887 195.0888 210.7457 206.2703 213.9690 110492118520
## CP065557 195.0887 195.0887 195.0888 211.4078 206.5918 213.6303  95188935619
## CP065558 195.0888 195.0887 195.0888 214.4299 213.6303 220.0277  69397375038
## CP076478 195.0886 195.0886 195.0887 211.4643 206.5617 214.0053 106924542624
## CP076479 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 215.3070 214.0053 220.3662  70803388266
## CP087256 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 210.8610 206.4086 213.6258 106234623067
## CP087257 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 215.0053 213.6258 220.9319  82648225465
## CP098054 195.0886 195.0885 195.0886 211.5346 206.4520 213.8643 104329095908
## CP098055 195.0886 195.0886 195.0887 215.1010 213.8643 220.3417  72008243674
## CP109249 195.0887 195.0886 195.0887 210.9312 206.1821 219.6389 160117551822
## CP120395 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 211.0796 206.0380 220.1115 162960175794
## CP131752 195.0886 195.0886 195.0887 211.1274 206.4629 220.0934 167271730925
## CP142775 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 210.8383 206.3606 213.6236 100791407062
## CP142776 195.0887 195.0886 195.0887 214.2553 213.6236 220.0807  67331463032
## CP153870 195.0887 195.0886 195.0888 211.3764 206.2850 219.9927 149281645822
## CP164662 195.0887 195.0886 195.0887 211.3226 206.2631 219.6486 153718999504
## CP175595 195.0887 195.0886 195.0887 211.4357 206.5142 213.9690  98131006471
## CP175596 195.0887 195.0886 195.0887 215.1370 213.9690 220.3543  64097631159
## CP186560 195.0887 195.0886 195.0888 210.9776 206.5679 213.6986  99251433306
## CP186561 195.0888 195.0887 195.0888 215.1503 213.6986 220.1468  67666084600
## CP197535 195.0888 195.0888 195.0889 211.3225 206.4815 213.9346 101248679160
## CP197536 195.0888 195.0887 195.0889 214.8461 213.9346 220.0631  61242264806
## CP208488 195.0883 195.0882 195.0883 210.9675 206.2562 213.8501 107443432322
## CP208489 195.0883 195.0883 195.0883 215.1598 213.8501 220.5496  72041918889
## CP219462 195.0885 195.0884 195.0885 211.4030 206.5637 214.3865 109483628416
## CP230272 195.0887 195.0886 195.0887 210.8972 206.5659 213.7478 104785336846
## CP230273 195.0886 195.0886 195.0887 215.0739 213.7478 220.0446  67734440731
## CP241261 195.0887 195.0887 195.0888 211.3203 206.4053 214.1663 108850767494
## CP241262 195.0887 195.0887 195.0888 215.4363 214.1663 220.4961  67339693729
##                  intb        maxo  sn sample peakwidth     mz.error ppm.error
## CP010851 103404574658 19912065024 148      1  7.159128 0.0008871450  4.547418
## CP010852  66666454867 15102042112 112      1  6.589830 0.0008901137  4.562635
## CP021684 102946002160 20498821120 129      2  7.412328 0.0008813964  4.517951
## CP021685  74589007260 16886339584 100      2  6.560430 0.0009337886  4.786508
## CP032582 198844812364 20352387072 117      3 16.934460 0.0009649531  4.946254
## CP032583  81538808949 16997617664  93      3  7.012470 0.0009243798  4.738279

## CP054605 109283657449 19892613120 142      5  7.698708 0.0010813619  5.542954
## CP065557  94149933851 18745135104 146      6  7.038498 0.0010604085  5.435549

## CP076478 105674325169 19863330816 123      7  7.443570 0.0009629135  4.935799
## CP076479  69632265967 15465336832  96      7  6.360924 0.0009501169  4.870205
## CP087256 104974196618 20064141312 119      8  7.217244 0.0009629808  4.936144
## CP087257  81261416734 16809177088  99      8  7.306044 0.0009459820  4.849010
## CP098054 103049527615 19357437952 123      9  7.412298 0.0009067759  4.648043
## CP098055  70787573557 15663849472 103      9  6.477402 0.0009567279  4.904092
## CP109249 158060509510 19002615808 134     10 13.456806 0.0010082618  5.168250
## CP120395 160768690208 19145445376 136     11 14.073576 0.0009670312  4.956906
## CP131752 164900624652 18687842304 116     12 13.630548 0.0009767237  5.006589
## CP142775  99608266669 19197122560 143     13  7.263024 0.0009648393  4.945670
## CP142776  66191043737 15633431552 123     13  6.457116 0.0010185950  5.221217
## CP153870 147257279480 17804779520 148     14 13.707750 0.0010354572  5.307651
## CP164662 151663078705 18895065088 145     15 13.385484 0.0010302821  5.281124
## CP175595  97077260750 19421229056 162     16  7.454796 0.0010233287  5.245481
## CP175596  63099859826 14803571712 123     16  6.385296 0.0010016284  5.134248
## CP186560  98183539000 19630155776 143     17  7.130706 0.0010932213  5.603744
## CP186561  66614981059 15734041600 114     17  6.448182 0.0011156627  5.718777
## CP197535 100195042582 19082620928 161     18  7.453116 0.0011496699  5.893094
## CP197536  60306732454 14315866112 140     18  6.128424 0.0011451879  5.870120
## CP208488 106685360008 19981119488 131     19  7.593882 0.0006430553  3.296238
## CP208489  71274584391 15839861760 104     19  6.699468 0.0006731896  3.450703
## CP219462 108536861882 19385874432 136     20  7.822842 0.0008175803  4.190836
## CP230272 103778189680 19647295488 138     21  7.181838 0.0010086151  5.170061
## CP230273  66767955921 15334866944 115     21  6.296796 0.0009910313  5.079928
## CP241261 107788732567 20744505344 145     22  7.761018 0.0010678963  5.473931
## CP241262  66375852851 15454690304 108     22  6.329832 0.0010809926  5.541061
peaks.pos |> filterMz(c(caffeine.mzr[1], caffeine.mzr[2])) |>
  filterRt(c(200, 250)) |> plot(type='XIC')

From the table, we can narrow down the ppm threshold to 6 and adjust the peakwidth.

cwp = CentWaveParam(
  ppm = 6,
  peakwidth = c(10, 30)
)

peaks.pos = findChromPeaks(pos.data |> filterRt(c(25, 480)), param = cwp)

chromPeaks(peaks.pos |> filterMz(c(caffeine.mzr[1], caffeine.mzr[2]))) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  mutate(
    peakwidth = rtmax - rtmin,
    mz.error = abs(mz - caffeine.mz),
    ppm.error = 10^6 * (mz.error / caffeine.mz)
  )
##                mz    mzmin    mzmax       rt    rtmin    rtmax         into
## CP004869 195.0885 195.0885 195.0886 212.2427 203.2291 223.1914 189736024712
## CP009667 195.0886 195.0885 195.0886 212.9368 203.4924 223.6761 195622205259
## CP014529 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 212.2637 203.0393 223.7884 215397787078
## CP019510 195.0887 195.0886 195.0888 212.9216 203.0630 223.5151 179951919189
## CP024470 195.0887 195.0887 195.0888 211.9769 202.9095 223.0533 187210400812
## CP029339 195.0887 195.0887 195.0888 212.6905 203.7254 222.8649 178305956941
## CP034208 195.0886 195.0886 195.0887 212.9201 203.2038 223.6599 196433378062
## CP039019 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 212.4086 203.0586 223.7803 206432208092
## CP043823 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 213.0207 203.1054 223.6413 192540860896
## CP048845 195.0887 195.0886 195.0887 212.4129 202.8213 223.4267 179019148583
## CP053824 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 212.5847 203.1645 223.4381 179800732740
## CP058904 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 212.8173 203.1138 223.8964 187527849683
## CP063857 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 212.3349 203.0109 222.9417 182173528646
## CP068833 195.0887 195.0886 195.0888 212.7514 202.9293 223.3206 163638503748
## CP073662 195.0887 195.0886 195.0888 212.5945 202.9085 222.9747 170342102635
## CP078582 195.0887 195.0886 195.0887 212.7647 203.6341 223.1921 173618364300
## CP083417 195.0888 195.0886 195.0888 212.4597 203.2241 222.9788 181658434927
## CP088309 195.0888 195.0888 195.0889 212.6225 203.1299 222.9177 175652493390
## CP093214 195.0883 195.0882 195.0884 212.6254 203.3811 223.4074 193803431055
## CP097994 195.0885 195.0883 195.0886 212.8236 203.2037 223.4241 185568823069
## CP102858 195.0887 195.0886 195.0887 212.3583 203.2143 223.3706 189893178123
## CP107745 195.0887 195.0887 195.0888 212.8479 203.5350 223.3287 190240542065
##                  intb        maxo  sn sample peakwidth     mz.error ppm.error
## CP004869 187148748717 19912065024 148      1  19.96222 0.0008834125  4.528285
## CP009667 192698175888 20498821120 129      2  20.18371 0.0009018712  4.622902
## CP014529 212019844154 20352387072 117      3  20.74911 0.0009496726  4.867928
## CP019510 176920879230 19273824256 128      4  20.45210 0.0010599625  5.433263
## CP024470 184238772534 19892613120 142      5  20.14376 0.0010774192  5.522744
## CP029339 175603817588 18745135104 146      6  19.13957 0.0010768877  5.520020
## CP034208 193203347582 19863330816 123      7  20.45610 0.0009573890  4.907481
## CP039019 203012549657 20064141312 119      8  20.72173 0.0009568089  4.904508
## CP043823 189199083260 19357437952 123      9  20.53589 0.0009290522  4.762230
## CP048845 176104375246 19002615808 134     10  20.60543 0.0009950463  5.100509
## CP053824 176840039102 19145445376 136     11  20.27368 0.0009737290  4.991238
## CP058904 184195196622 18687842304 116     12  20.78261 0.0009853535  5.050825
## CP063857 179110395634 19197122560 143     13  19.93077 0.0009883035  5.065946
## CP068833 160828482822 17804779520 148     14  20.39134 0.0010552844  5.409283
## CP073662 167464376330 18895065088 145     15  20.06615 0.0010392947  5.327321
## CP078582 170951151365 19421229056 162     16  19.55804 0.0010120924  5.187885
## CP083417 178870931121 19630155776 143     17  19.75471 0.0010953336  5.614571
## CP088309 173010338646 19082620928 161     18  19.78777 0.0011482684  5.885910
## CP093214 191910973172 19981119488 131     19  20.02634 0.0006639621  3.403404
## CP097994 183292658855 19385874432 136     20  20.22038 0.0008279205  4.243839
## CP102858 187249870640 19647295488 138     21  20.15620 0.0009967268  5.109123
## CP107745 187635741466 20744505344 145     22  19.79370 0.0010752796  5.511777
peaks.pos |> filterMz(c(caffeine.mzr[1], caffeine.mzr[2])) |>
  filterRt(c(200, 250)) |> plot(type='XIC')

par(mfrow=c(1,1))

peaks.pos |> 
  filterMz(c(caffeine.mzr[1], caffeine.mzr[2])) |>
  chromatogram() |> 
  plot(col = caffeine.colors, 
    peakBg = caffeine.peakBg)

This looks better, but the integration window looks problematic. Caffeine tends to tail in positive ESI mode for reasons unknown, and that tail isn’t captured here. It’s probably fine since it’s consistent, but we can adjust cwp to fix it by changing the default parameter integrate = 1 to integrate = 2.

cwp = CentWaveParam(
  ppm = 6,
  peakwidth = c(10, 30),
  integrate = 2
)

peaks.pos = findChromPeaks(pos.data |> filterRt(c(25, 480)), param = cwp)

chromPeaks(peaks.pos |> filterMz(c(caffeine.mzr[1], caffeine.mzr[2]))) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  mutate(
    peakwidth = rtmax - rtmin,
    mz.error = abs(mz - caffeine.mz),
    ppm.error = 10^6 * (mz.error / caffeine.mz)
  )
##                mz    mzmin    mzmax       rt    rtmin    rtmax         into
## CP004880 195.0885 195.0885 195.0886 212.2427 203.2291 229.4070 204523465378
## CP009688 195.0886 195.0885 195.0886 212.9368 205.4037 229.3856 206064500106
## CP014562 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 212.2637 206.8788 229.5019 223336128524
## CP019547 195.0887 195.0886 195.0888 212.9216 205.9339 229.2153 188103888475
## CP024510 195.0887 195.0887 195.0888 211.9769 202.9095 247.3555 222551402618
## CP029396 195.0887 195.0887 195.0888 212.6905 207.5472 258.1505 221642014549
## CP034265 195.0886 195.0886 195.0887 212.9201 205.6021 229.3348 206025318950
## CP039088 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 212.4086 206.8883 229.9703 215521286261
## CP043904 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 213.0207 205.9757 227.9214 198106611149
## CP048937 195.0887 195.0886 195.0887 212.4129 203.3014 230.5636 190223866608
## CP053927 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 212.5847 205.0816 228.6810 187736994857
## CP059016 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 212.8173 205.9875 244.3877 213989644207
## CP063980 195.0886 195.0885 195.0887 212.3349 205.4007 252.5123 219012003529
## CP068965 195.0887 195.0886 195.0888 212.7514 205.3197 227.6065 168846366767
## CP073806 195.0887 195.0886 195.0888 212.5945 204.8225 228.6781 178575478208
## CP078739 195.0887 195.0886 195.0887 212.7647 206.5142 228.4279 180805060020
## CP083583 195.0888 195.0886 195.0888 212.4597 205.6119 241.5689 211020453468
## CP088480 195.0888 195.0888 195.0889 212.6225 205.0425 234.3146 193560327004
## CP093397 195.0883 195.0882 195.0884 212.6254 206.2562 231.9617 208956971265
## CP098188 195.0885 195.0883 195.0886 212.8236 204.6442 228.6565 194885650781
## CP103054 195.0887 195.0886 195.0887 212.3583 206.0866 231.9371 203844104530
## CP107954 195.0887 195.0887 195.0888 212.8479 206.4053 271.0174 244939818293
##                  intb        maxo  sn sample peakwidth     mz.error ppm.error
## CP004880 201078720296 19912065024 148      1  26.17784 0.0008834125  4.528285
## CP009688 202387443951 20498821120 129      2  23.98184 0.0009018712  4.622902
## CP014562 219308557948 20352387072 117      3  22.62311 0.0009496726  4.867928
## CP019547 184396237723 19273824256 128      4  23.28142 0.0010599625  5.433263
## CP024510 215714077972 19892613120 142      5  44.44601 0.0010774192  5.522744
## CP029396 213954188495 18745135104 146      6  50.60331 0.0010768877  5.520020
## CP034265 202033631508 19863330816 123      7  23.73275 0.0009573890  4.907481
## CP039088 211369046275 20064141312 119      8  23.08194 0.0009568089  4.904508
## CP043904 194286800796 19357437952 123      9  21.94568 0.0009290522  4.762230
## CP048937 186243674845 19002615808 134     10  27.26215 0.0009950463  5.100509
## CP053927 184100017832 19145445376 136     11  23.59939 0.0009737290  4.991238
## CP059016 207285599439 18687842304 116     12  38.40017 0.0009853535  5.050825
## CP063980 211284548089 19197122560 143     13  47.11169 0.0009883035  5.065946
## CP068965 165602388365 17804779520 148     14  22.28677 0.0010552844  5.409283
## CP073806 174988115520 18895065088 145     15  23.85567 0.0010392947  5.327321
## CP078739 177630957671 19421229056 162     16  21.91372 0.0010120924  5.187885
## CP083583 205593502608 19630155776 143     17  35.95700 0.0010953336  5.614571
## CP088480 189461300791 19082620928 161     18  29.27207 0.0011482684  5.885910
## CP093397 206244419912 19981119488 131     19  25.70552 0.0006639621  3.403404
## CP098188 192031214841 19385874432 136     20  24.01236 0.0008279205  4.243839
## CP103054 200183417124 19647295488 138     21  25.85047 0.0009967268  5.109123
## CP107954 235691352381 20744505344 145     22  64.61208 0.0010752796  5.511777
peaks.pos |> filterMz(c(caffeine.mzr[1], caffeine.mzr[2])) |>
  filterRt(c(200, 250)) |> plot(type='XIC')

par(mfrow=c(1,1))

peaks.pos |> 
  filterMz(c(caffeine.mzr[1], caffeine.mzr[2])) |>
  chromatogram() |> 
  plot(col = caffeine.colors, 
    peakBg = caffeine.peakBg)

This looks worse because it’s not integrating the tail consistently. I’ll settle on the previous parameters.

cwp = CentWaveParam(
  ppm = 6,
  peakwidth = c(10, 30)
)

peaks.pos = findChromPeaks(pos.data |> filterRt(c(25, 480)), param = cwp)

Another good control for quality is to look at random peaks to see how those were treated.

par(mfrow=c(3,3))

set.seed(17)
sample(seq_len(nrow(chromPeaks(peaks.pos))),9)
## [1]  50910 100847  12263  12711  65686  54401  82694 100024 105349
chromPeaks(peaks.pos)[sample(seq_len(nrow(chromPeaks(peaks.pos))),9)[1],]
##           mz        mzmin        mzmax           rt        rtmin        rtmax 
##     259.1091     259.1085     259.1097     323.6425     320.7745     326.5078 
##         into         intb         maxo           sn       sample 
## 4790596.0300 4780756.1584 1636288.0000      66.0000      15.0000
sampleData(peaks.pos)[7,]
## DataFrame with 1 row and 40 columns
##                      Sample.Name Protocol.REF Parameter.Value.Post.Extraction.
##                      <character>  <character>                      <character>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML POS_2_year...   Extraction                    MeOH/H2O(4...
##                    Parameter.Value.Derivatization.  Extract.Name Protocol.REF.1
##                                        <character>   <character>    <character>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML                            none POS_7_year...  Chromatogr...
##                    Parameter.Value.Chromatography.Instrument. Term.Source.REF
##                                                   <character>     <character>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML                              Thermo Sci...           MTBLS
##                    Term.Accession.Number Parameter.Value.Autosampler.model.
##                              <character>                          <logical>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML         http://www...                                 NA
##                    Parameter.Value.Column.model. Parameter.Value.Column.type.
##                                      <character>                  <character>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML                 ACQUITY UP...                reverse ph...
##                    Parameter.Value.Guard.column. Labeled.Extract.Name     Label
##                                      <character>            <logical> <logical>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML                 ACQUITY UP...                   NA        NA
##                    Term.Source.REF.1 Term.Accession.Number.1 Protocol.REF.2
##                            <logical>               <logical>    <character>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML                NA                      NA  Mass spect...
##                    Parameter.Value.Scan.polarity.
##                                       <character>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML                  alternatin...
##                    Parameter.Value.Scan.m.z.range. Parameter.Value.Instrument.
##                                        <character>                 <character>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML                         70-1050               Thermo Sci...
##                    Term.Source.REF.2 Term.Accession.Number.2
##                          <character>             <character>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML             MTBLS           http://www...
##                    Parameter.Value.Ion.source. Term.Source.REF.3
##                                    <character>       <character>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML               ion spray ...               FIX
##                    Term.Accession.Number.3 Parameter.Value.Mass.analyzer.
##                                <character>                    <character>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML           http://pur...                       orbitrap
##                    Term.Source.REF.4 Term.Accession.Number.4 MS.Assay.Name
##                          <character>             <character>   <character>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML                MS           http://pur... POS_7_year...
##                    Raw.Spectral.Data.File Protocol.REF.3 Normalization.Name
##                               <character>    <character>        <character>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML          FILES/POS_...  Data trans...      POS_7_year...
##                    Derived.Spectral.Data.File Protocol.REF.4
##                                   <character>    <character>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML              FILES/POS_...  Metabolite...
##                    Data.Transformation.Name Metabolite.Assignment.File
##                                 <character>                <character>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML            POS_7_year...              m_MTBLS133...
##                       polarity      year spectraOrigin
##                    <character> <numeric>   <character>
## POS_2_years_1.mzML         POS         2 /home/mori...
year.colors
##        00        02        05        07        09        11        NA 
## "#4B0055" "#274983" "#008298" "#00B28A" "#7ED357" "#FDE333" "#7f7f7f"
sampleData(peaks.pos)$year
##  [1]  0  0  0 11 11 11  2  2  2  5  5  5  7  7  7  9  9  9 NA NA NA NA
sort(sampleData(peaks.pos)$year, na.last = TRUE)
##  [1]  0  0  0  2  2  2  5  5  5  7  7  7  9  9  9 11 11 11 NA NA NA NA
sample.colors = year.colors[str_replace_na(sprintf('%02d',sort(sampleData(peaks.pos)$year, na.last = TRUE)))]
sample.colors
##        00        00        00        02        02        02        05        05 
## "#4B0055" "#4B0055" "#4B0055" "#274983" "#274983" "#274983" "#008298" "#008298" 
##        05        07        07        07        09        09        09        11 
## "#008298" "#00B28A" "#00B28A" "#00B28A" "#7ED357" "#7ED357" "#7ED357" "#FDE333" 
##        11        11        NA        NA        NA        NA 
## "#FDE333" "#FDE333" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f"
set.seed(17)
sample.colors[chromPeaks(peaks.pos)[sample(seq_len(nrow(chromPeaks(peaks.pos))),9)[1], 'sample']]
##        07 
## "#00B28A"
sample.colors[chromPeaks(peaks.pos |>
  filterMz(c(caffeine.mzr[1], caffeine.mzr[2])))[,'sample']]
##        00        00        00        02        02        02        05        05 
## "#4B0055" "#4B0055" "#4B0055" "#274983" "#274983" "#274983" "#008298" "#008298" 
##        05        07        07        07        09        09        09        11 
## "#008298" "#00B28A" "#00B28A" "#00B28A" "#7ED357" "#7ED357" "#7ED357" "#FDE333" 
##        11        11        NA        NA        NA        NA 
## "#FDE333" "#FDE333" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f"
par(mfrow=c(3,3))
walk(sample(seq_len(nrow(chromPeaks(peaks.pos))), 9), ~ {

  peak.info = chromPeaks(peaks.pos)[.x,]
  test.colors = sample.colors[str_replace_na(sprintf('%02d', sampleData(peaks.pos)$year))]

  # Since we filterFile() down to a single sample below, col/peakBg need to
  # be a single color matching that one sample, not the full 22-sample
  # vector (a length mismatch there makes xcms fall back to default gray).
  peak.color = test.colors[peak.info['sample']]

  peaks.pos |>
    filterFile(peak.info['sample']) |>
    filterRt(c(peak.info['rtmin'] - 30, peak.info['rtmax'] + 30)) |>
    filterMz(c(peak.info['mzmin'], peak.info['mzmax'])) |>
  chromatogram() |>
  plot(col = peak.color, peakBg = paste0(peak.color, '70'))
})

Most of these look reasonable compared to a perfect Gaussian peak. It’s never going to be perfect with automated analysis. In the Kumler article, there are new metrics for peak quality that use a peak correlation metric and signal to noise estimate to filter out peaks, but I’ll save those for another time.

Peak grouping/correspondence

Each chromPeak is specific to a single sample. They need to be grouped across samples to compare. Tracking peaks across samples is called “correspondence” in xcms.

Note, usually people perform retention time alignment before correspondence. In my opinion, if you need to align retention times, your chromatography is not robust enough and you’re not generating good data. Sometimes this is the case when sequences don’t use at least a few equlibration injections at the beginning. This particular study looks well-behaved.

pdp = PeakDensityParam(
  sampleGroups = sampleData(peaks.pos)$year,
  minFraction = 0.5,
  bw = 5
)
caffeine.peaks = peaks.pos |> filterMz(c(caffeine.mzr[1], caffeine.mzr[2]))

plotChromPeakDensity(chromatogram(caffeine.peaks), 
  col = sample.colors, 
  peakBg = paste0(sample.colors[chromPeaks(caffeine.peaks)[,'sample']], '05'), 
  param =  pdp)

That example looks pretty good so we’ll apply the same parameters to the full dataset.

nrow(chromPeaks(peaks.pos))
## [1] 107756
peaks.pos.grouped = groupChromPeaks(peaks.pos, param = pdp)
nrow(featureDefinitions(peaks.pos.grouped))
## [1] 5780

We’ve now condensed the data from over 100,000 peaks to 5780 features, about 18-fold, which makes sense since there are 22 samples. Let’s plot some random features to see how well it worked.

set.seed(17)
rand.features = featureChromatograms(
  peaks.pos.grouped, 
  features = sample(seq_len(nrow(featureDefinitions(peaks.pos.grouped))), 9)
)

featureDefinitions(rand.features)
## DataFrame with 9 rows and 16 columns
##            mzmed     mzmin     mzmax     rtmed     rtmin     rtmax    npeaks
##        <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <numeric>
## FT1970   271.228   271.228   271.228   389.475   388.302   390.049        19
## FT1758   253.091   253.091   253.092   272.539   271.918   273.184        18
## FT2156   287.104   287.075   287.150   264.409   257.588   265.312        43
## FT2543   319.216   319.191   319.228   391.261   375.558   396.557        54
## FT5367   735.243   735.242   735.243   350.050   349.933   350.196         7
## FT4071   504.196   504.195   504.197   296.720   295.974   297.191        17
## FT1965   271.166   271.145   271.167   322.563   312.371   322.997        22
## FT4519   573.306   573.305   573.306   459.321   458.787   460.089        21
## FT3882   477.304   477.282   477.393   389.635   373.483   392.113        51
##               X0        X2        X5        X7        X9       X11
##        <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <numeric>
## FT1970         0         3         3         3         3         3
## FT1758         3         3         3         2         2         1
## FT2156         3         3         3         3         3         3
## FT2543         3         3         3         3         3         3
## FT5367         0         0         1         2         2         2
## FT4071         3         3         3         3         0         1
## FT1965         2         2         1         3         2         3
## FT4519         3         3         2         3         3         3
## FT3882         3         3         3         3         3         3
##                peakidx  ms_level       row
##                 <list> <integer> <integer>
## FT1970       1,2,3,...         1         1
## FT1758    20,21,22,...         1         2
## FT2156    38,39,40,...         1         3
## FT2543    81,82,83,...         1         4
## FT5367 135,136,137,...         1         5
## FT4071 142,143,144,...         1         6
## FT1965 159,160,161,...         1         7
## FT4519 181,182,183,...         1         8
## FT3882 202,203,204,...         1         9
featureValues(rand.features)

## FT1758          11765111          10629631           7562480
## FT2156           6399067           4513905           4107090

## FT2156             4138671             4297856             2490667

## FT3882           15907875           12022989           11849041

## FT1970           33271766           34354602           42094843
## FT1758           11159653            8708467            8237912

## FT2543           14344786           85891191           13014478

## FT1965           67186959                 NA                 NA
## FT4519           18012744                 NA           22479202

## FT1970           53936498           77281779           69360537
## FT1758                 NA            4491325            6433093
## FT2156            1747443            5058607            5539149
## FT2543           70711519            6996551            6207820
## FT5367                 NA           14679598            6944847

## FT4519           23260746           18278702           19873172
## FT3882            9641633           33476800           24289797
##        POS_9_years_1.mzML POS_9_years_2.mzML POS_9_years_3.mzML
## FT1970           47649602           40868678           34668516
## FT1758                 NA           10420814            7733945
## FT2156            2794442            3705168            3525536
## FT2543            9070810           22481261            7570147
## FT5367            3238292                 NA            4459848
## FT4071                 NA                 NA                 NA
## FT1965                 NA           44197346           30920873
## FT4519           23025421           23721024           20990850
## FT3882           11005085           47140227           31884917
##        POS_Hh12LWL_QC01.mzML POS_Hh12LWL_QC02.mzML POS_Hh12LWL_QC03.mzML
## FT1970              45146660              40405725              43462801
## FT1758               8749008               7676339               7536304
## FT2156               2497812               6937127               2942619
## FT2543              11291232               9540436              77124004
## FT5367                    NA                    NA                    NA
## FT4071               5448546               6138798               5283464
## FT1965              63832497              58543716              57093425
## FT4519              81294378              50996788              19222285
## FT3882              14217033              12395260              11554949
##        POS_Hh12LWL_QC04.mzML
## FT1970              43889246
## FT1758               6237141
## FT2156               2910318
## FT2543              10061434
## FT5367                    NA
## FT4071               5800901
## FT1965                    NA
## FT4519              47873058
## FT3882              14514082
par(mfrow=c(3,3))
plot(rand.features, col = sample.colors,
  peakBg = paste0(sample.colors[chromPeaks(rand.features)[,'sample']], '10'))

xcms might not have integrated some features, so we have to fill in missing peaks with “gap filling.”

peaks.pos.grouped.filled = fillChromPeaks(peaks.pos.grouped, param = ChromPeakAreaParam())

rand.features.filled = featureChromatograms(
  peaks.pos.grouped.filled,
  features = rownames(featureDefinitions(rand.features))
)
featureValues(rand.features.filled[7,])
##        POS_0_year_1.mzML POS_0_year_2.mzML POS_0_year_3.mzML
## FT1965          86375846         133463459          66746938
##        POS_11_years_1.mzML POS_11_years_2.mzML POS_11_years_3.mzML
## FT1965            47362067            39474894             4336810
##        POS_2_years_1.mzML POS_2_years_2.mzML POS_2_years_3.mzML
## FT1965            1656675           76811242           69439500
##        POS_5_years_1.mzML POS_5_years_2.mzML POS_5_years_3.mzML
## FT1965           67186959           46582700           60018210
##        POS_7_years_1.mzML POS_7_years_2.mzML POS_7_years_3.mzML
## FT1965            2238103           54021337           50736792
##        POS_9_years_1.mzML POS_9_years_2.mzML POS_9_years_3.mzML
## FT1965           49111992           44197346           30920873
##        POS_Hh12LWL_QC01.mzML POS_Hh12LWL_QC02.mzML POS_Hh12LWL_QC03.mzML
## FT1965              63832497              58543716              57093425
##        POS_Hh12LWL_QC04.mzML
## FT1965              58588763
plot(rand.features.filled,
  col = sample.colors,
  peakBg = paste0(sample.colors[chromPeaks(rand.features.filled)[,'sample']], '10'))

saveRDS(peaks.pos.grouped.filled, '_rds/peaks_pos_grouped_filled.rds')

From the darker peak backgrounds, we can see that more peaks were integrated with this method. This helps prevent zero’d integrations from skewing the downstream analysis.

Using the final results

We now basically have a table of feature intensities grouped across samples, which proxies for metabolite abundances. We can use this for downstream statistical analyses to find what are the important features that change with storage time. This is a lot less work than trying to identify every single peak and then finding out most of them weren’t important.

I usually start with a principal component analysis (PCA) to get an overview of the data.

pos.results = quantify(peaks.pos.grouped.filled, value = 'into', method = 'sum')
# ropls's plot.opls() does as.vector(parAsColFcVn) then re-derives its own
# factor internally, discarding any custom level order we set. So instead of
# pre-building a factor, we pass a plain character vector whose labels are
# zero-padded to match names(year.colors) exactly (see above) - that way
# ropls's own alphabetical re-sort lands on the same order as year.colors.
year.groups = ifelse(is.na(colData(pos.results)$year), 'NA',
  sprintf('%02d', colData(pos.results)$year))

pca.pos = opls(assay(pos.results, 'raw') |> t())
## PCA
## 22 samples x 5780 variables
## standard scaling of predictors
## 3040 (2%) NAs
##       R2X(cum) pre ort
## Total    0.518   3   0

plot(pca.pos, parPaletteVc = year.colors, parAsColFcVn = year.groups)

Nice. Each year condition clusters together, with the QC samples (prepared by mixing equal amounts of each sample) in the middle. The first principal component explains 27% of the variance, with two features, FT4735 and FT5462, heavily loaded on opposite sides of the p1 loading plot. On the score plot, we see short-term storage samples on the right and long-term storage samples on the left, suggesting a linear relationship between storage time and metabolite abundances.

We can go back to the feature definitions table to identify the two standout features using rowData() from SummarizedExperiment.

rowData(pos.results)[c('FT4735', 'FT5462'),]
## DataFrame with 2 rows and 14 columns
##            mzmed     mzmin     mzmax     rtmed     rtmin     rtmax    npeaks
##        <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <numeric>
## FT4735   601.491   601.482   601.501   449.964   448.039   451.889         2
## FT5462   764.553   764.551   764.555   437.017   436.797   437.308         5
##                0         2         5         7         9        11  ms_level
##        <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <integer>
## FT4735         0         0         2         0         0         0         1
## FT5462         0         0         2         0         0         0         1
big.feature.chromatograms = featureChromatograms(peaks.pos.grouped.filled, features = c('FT4735', 'FT5462'))

plot(big.feature.chromatograms,
  col = sample.colors,
  peakBg = paste0(sample.colors[chromPeaks(big.feature.chromatograms)[,'sample']], '10'))

sample.colors
##        00        00        00        02        02        02        05        05 
## "#4B0055" "#4B0055" "#4B0055" "#274983" "#274983" "#274983" "#008298" "#008298" 
##        05        07        07        07        09        09        09        11 
## "#008298" "#00B28A" "#00B28A" "#00B28A" "#7ED357" "#7ED357" "#7ED357" "#FDE333" 
##        11        11        NA        NA        NA        NA 
## "#FDE333" "#FDE333" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f" "#7f7f7f"
sample.colors[1]
##        00 
## "#4B0055"
sample.colors[22]
##        NA 
## "#7f7f7f"
chromPeaks(big.feature.chromatograms)[,'sample']
## CP053471 CP058525 CP048347 CP126936 CP058407 CP092699 CP142912 CP102377 
##       11       12       10       11       12       19       20       21 
## CP107258 
##       22

These features look pretty noisy and probably shouldn’t be loaded so heavily. Let’s try a different scaling method, pareto scaling, which is often used in biological data.

pca.pos.pareto = opls(assay(pos.results, 'raw') |> t(), scaleC='pareto')
## PCA
## 22 samples x 5780 variables
## pareto scaling of predictors
## 3040 (2%) NAs
##       R2X(cum) pre ort
## Total    0.554   2   0

plot(pca.pos.pareto, parPaletteVc = year.colors, parAsColFcVn = year.groups)

You can see that scaling has a big effect on the results. The first principal component now explains 41% of the variance, and we get more standout features. Let’s see how those look.

important.features = getLoadingMN(pca.pos.pareto) |> as.data.frame() |>
  arrange(desc(abs(p1))) |>
  dplyr::slice(1:9) |>
  rownames()
important.features
## [1] "FT0930" "FT0931" "FT0429" "FT1143" "FT0762" "FT0836" "FT4766" "FT0304"
## [9] "FT0396"
important.feature.chromatograms = featureChromatograms(peaks.pos.grouped.filled,
  features = important.features)

plot(important.feature.chromatograms,
  col = sample.colors,
  peakBg = paste0(sample.colors[chromPeaks(important.feature.chromatograms)[,'sample']], '10'))

These look much more reasonable as important peaks, and we can clearly see differences in metabolite abundances when coloring by storage time. Some may be more noisy but some degree of false positives must be expected.

Notice that some of the features are actually redundant, or degenerate, meaning they originate from the same compound or metabolite. In particular, the top left and middle feature show the distinct peak shape of isomers coeluting, and they elute at the same retention time. The middle right and bottom middle features also overlap. Another way to reduce the dataset is to group these degenerate features into compounds through the CAMERA package, which groups coeluting features into “pseudospectra.” However, this package hasn’t been updated to work with the new xcms interfaces introduced with version 3.0, so I haven’t touched it in a while. Another, more recent, package is MsFeatures by Johannes Rainer. This one looks promising, and since it’s so new I haven’t gotten to implement it yet. But the ideas behind it sound strong, as it groups features by how well they correlate in the retention time dimension.

I digress. My point is that a feature is not a compound, but once we get from features to compounds, data size can be reduced immensely.

Since this is a longitudinal study, we can use year as the independent variable and feature intensities as dependent variables. We can use a linear model to see how the important features change with storage time.

lm.results = map_dfr(important.features, ~ {
  feature.intensities = assay(pos.results, 'raw')[.x,]
  lm.fit = lm(feature.intensities ~ colData(pos.results)$year)
  broom::tidy(lm.fit) |>
    mutate(feature = .x) |>
    mutate(
      adj.r.squared = broom::glance(lm.fit)$adj.r.squared
    )
})

lm.results
## # A tibble: 18 × 7
##    term              estimate std.error statistic  p.value feature adj.r.squared
##    <chr>                <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl> <chr>           <dbl>
##  1 (Intercept)        1.24e11    9.63e9     12.9  7.51e-10 FT0930          0.383
##  2 colData(pos.resu… -4.79e 9    1.41e9     -3.40 3.66e- 3 FT0930          0.383
##  3 (Intercept)        7.60e10    2.61e9     29.1  2.77e-15 FT0931          0.567
##  4 colData(pos.resu… -1.84e 9    3.82e8     -4.82 1.87e- 4 FT0931          0.567
##  5 (Intercept)        4.15e10    2.90e9     14.3  1.55e-10 FT0429          0.465
##  6 colData(pos.resu… -1.68e 9    4.24e8     -3.97 1.09e- 3 FT0429          0.465
##  7 (Intercept)        2.01e11    4.07e9     49.4  6.38e-19 FT1143          0.421
##  8 colData(pos.resu… -2.18e 9    5.96e8     -3.65 2.14e- 3 FT1143          0.421
##  9 (Intercept)        3.16e10    2.69e9     11.7  2.90e- 9 FT0762          0.457
## 10 colData(pos.resu… -1.54e 9    3.94e8     -3.91 1.25e- 3 FT0762          0.457
## 11 (Intercept)        5.09e10    2.63e9     19.3  1.60e-12 FT0836          0.485
## 12 colData(pos.resu… -1.59e 9    3.85e8     -4.12 7.98e- 4 FT0836          0.485
## 13 (Intercept)        1.76e10    1.32e9     13.4  4.27e-10 FT4766          0.766
## 14 colData(pos.resu… -1.45e 9    1.93e8     -7.52 1.23e- 6 FT4766          0.766
## 15 (Intercept)        2.84e10    1.48e9     19.2  1.79e-12 FT0304          0.491
## 16 colData(pos.resu… -9.03e 8    2.17e8     -4.17 7.26e- 4 FT0304          0.491
## 17 (Intercept)        8.25e 9    1.00e9      8.21 3.97e- 7 FT0396          0.703
## 18 colData(pos.resu…  9.44e 8    1.47e8      6.42 8.50e- 6 FT0396          0.703
important.features.high.r2 = lm.results |> filter(adj.r.squared > 0.7) |> pull(feature) |> unique()

This narrowed it down to two features with an adjusted R-squared greater than 0.7, FT4766 and FT0396, or the plots corresponding to the 607.2477_411 m/z-RT and 130.0507_69 m/z-RT features above.

Let’s see how they quantitatively changed with storage time.

map(important.features.high.r2, ~ {
  feature.intensities = assay(pos.results, 'raw')[.x,]

  plot.data = tibble(
    feature = .x,
    year = colData(pos.results)$year,
    intensity = feature.intensities
  )

  ggplot(plot.data, aes(x = year, y = intensity)) +
    geom_point() +
    geom_boxplot(aes(group = factor(year))) +
    geom_smooth(method = 'lm', color = 'red') +
    labs(title = paste0('Feature ', .x, ' vs. Storage Time'),
      x = 'Storage Time (years)',
      y = 'Feature Intensity') +
    theme_classic(base_size = 16)
})
## [[1]]

## 
## [[2]]

So now we can say something smart about how storage time affects the metabolite abundances in white tea. Feature FT4766 decreases with storage time, while FT0396 increases, with p-values of 1.23e-6 and 8.50e-6, respectively.

Quantitatively, FT0396 just about doubled over 11 years, and FT4766 decreased to about 20% of its original abundance. This is a pretty big change, and it would be interesting to see what these compounds are. We can try to identify them by looking up their m/z values in metabolite databases.

Looking up the m/z from FT4766 across Phytohub, Massbank of Europe, and Massbank of North America, only Phytohub gave a potential match for ligstroside via an [M+2MeCN+H]+ adduct, while the other two yielded no results. We can check this to see if the protonated ion, [M+H]+, coincides with that feature.

ligstroside.mzr = LCMStools::calcPPM(524.1894+1.00728, 100)
featureDefinitions(peaks.pos.grouped.filled |> 
  filterMz(c(ligstroside.mzr[1], ligstroside.mzr[2])))
##           mzmed    mzmin    mzmax    rtmed    rtmin    rtmax npeaks 0 2 5 7 9
## FT4188 525.1777 525.1768 525.1781 359.1033 358.6771 359.6962     22 3 3 3 3 3
## FT4189 525.1775 525.1773 525.1777 323.8373 322.7189 333.7480      6 2 2 2 0 0
##        11      peakidx ms_level
## FT4188  3 1, 2, 4,....        1
## FT4189  0 3, 5, 10....        1

The resulting two filtered features don’t match the retention time of the ion, so this is likely something not in the databases.

For FT0396, several matches were found in Phytohub as [M+2H] adducts. We can check if this makes sense by looking for an isotope peak at + 0.5 m/z units away from 130.0507, corresponding to where the 1C13 isotope would be if doubly protonated.

c13.mass.diff = 13.00335 - 12
c13.mass.diff
## [1] 1.00335
ft0396.mzr = LCMStools::calcPPM(
  featureDefinitions(peaks.pos.grouped.filled)['FT0396', 'mzmed'] + c13.mass.diff/2, 100
)

featureDefinitions(peaks.pos.grouped.filled |>
  filterMz(c(ft0396.mzr[1], ft0396.mzr[2])))
##  [1] mzmed    mzmin    mzmax    rtmed    rtmin    rtmax    npeaks   0       
##  [9] 2        5        7        9        11       peakidx  ms_level
## <0 rows> (or 0-length row.names)

This also yielded no features, so it’s likely a mismatch. Metabolite identification is a difficult problem, and these two compounds would likely need some structural information determined from MS/MS, or to be isolated with multidimensional chromatography or fractionating sample preparation, then analyzed by NMR to confirm their structures.

There are many other ways to analyze this data, and that may be a topic for a future post. For now, this is a good starting point for exploring longitudinal metabolomics data.

sessionInfo()

References

12

The dataset is described in , using the repository infrastructure described by .

Raw LC-MS data used in this post is from the MetaboLights study MTBLS13336 Shu Ya Wei and Long Wen Li, available under CC0 1.0 Universal at https://www.ebi.ac.uk/metabolights/MTBLS13336. MetaboLights: Yurekten et al., 2023.

(1)
Smith, C. A.; Want, E. J.; O’Maille, G.; Abagyan, R.; Siuzdak, G. XCMS:  Processing Mass Spectrometry Data for Metabolite Profiling Using Nonlinear Peak Alignment, Matching, and Identification. Analytical Chemistry 2006, 78 (3), 779–787. https://doi.org/10.1021/ac051437y.
(2)
Louail, P.; Brunius, C.; Garcia-Aloy, M.; Kumler, W.; Storz, N.; Stanstrup, J.; Treutler, H.; Vangeenderhuysen, P.; Witting, M.; Neumann, S.; Rainer, J. Xcms in Peak Form: Now Anchoring a Complete Metabolomics Data Preprocessing and Analysis Software Ecosystem. Analytical Chemistry 2025, 97 (50), 27639–27645. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.5c04338.
